sourceTitle: Synced Databases bridge the gap between different tools

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using Synced Databases in Notion?

options.text: They allow you to edit Jira tickets directly within Notion.
options.explanation: Synced Databases are read‑only; edits must be made in the original tool.

options.text: They bring information from external tools like Jira and GitHub into Notion as up‑to‑date databases.
options.explanation: Correct – Synced Databases import data from external tools and keep it current.

options.text: They automatically duplicate all Notion pages into GitHub repositories.
options.explanation: Synced Databases only pull data from external tools; they do not push Notion pages to GitHub.

options.text: They replace the need for any external project‑management tools.
options.explanation: Synced Databases complement, not replace, tools like Jira and GitHub.

questions.question: Which step is required before you can paste a Jira board link as a Synced Database in Notion?

options.text: Create a new Notion page and select "Import" from the menu.
options.explanation: Importing is not needed; you paste the link directly after authentication.

options.text: Authenticate Jira under Settings → My connected apps.
options.explanation: You must first connect and authenticate Jira before syncing.

options.text: Enable the "Write‑back" option in the Jira integration settings.
options.explanation: Synced Databases are read‑only; there is no write‑back option.

options.text: Install a third‑party Notion‑Jira Chrome extension.
options.explanation: No external extension is required; native Notion integration handles it.

questions.question: What happens when you edit a field of a Jira issue directly in the Synced Database view?

options.text: The change is saved in Notion and overwrites the original Jira issue.
options.explanation: Synced Databases are read‑only; edits are not saved back to Jira.

options.text: Notion shows an error and prevents the edit.
options.explanation: Notion allows the edit UI but does not push changes; the edit is discarded.

options.text: The edit is ignored and the original Jira data remains unchanged.
options.explanation: Because the database is read‑only, any edit attempt is ignored.

options.text: The edit creates a duplicate issue in Notion without affecting Jira.
options.explanation: No duplicate is created; the data stays synced with the source.

questions.question: How can you automatically create a relation between a GitHub Pull Request and a Notion page?

options.text: Add a Relation property, toggle on "Automatically Relate", and include the Notion page link in the PR description.
options.explanation: Auto‑relations work when the PR description or title contains a link to a Notion page.

options.text: Manually drag the Pull Request into the Notion page using the Notion sidebar.
options.explanation: Relations must be set up via properties; dragging is not a feature.

options.text: Enable "Sync Back" in the GitHub integration settings.
options.explanation: "Sync Back" does not exist; relations are created via the auto‑relate toggle.

options.text: Create a new GitHub webhook that posts to Notion every time a PR is opened.
options.explanation: Webhooks are not required for the built‑in auto‑relation feature.

questions.question: Which Notion view type would be most appropriate to visualize the status of tasks from a Jira sprint?

options.text: Calendar view grouped by due date.
options.explanation: Calendar view shows dates, not status columns.

options.text: Board view grouped by the "Status" property.
options.explanation: A board view grouped by Status mimics a Kanban board for sprint tracking.

options.text: List view sorted alphabetically by task name.
options.explanation: Sorting alphabetically does not convey status progression.

options.text: Gallery view showing task thumbnails.
options.explanation: Gallery view is visual but not suited for status organization.

questions.question: What is required to make a Rollup property work after establishing a Relation between two databases?

options.text: Select the Relation property, choose the target property to roll up, and define the calculation.
options.explanation: Rollups need the relation, the property to aggregate, and a calculation method.

options.text: Enable "Write‑back" on the source database.
options.explanation: Write‑back is not a concept for rollups; they are read‑only aggregations.

options.text: Create a duplicate of the target database inside the source database.
options.explanation: Duplication is unnecessary; rollups reference the related database directly.

options.text: Add a formula property that mirrors the target property’s value.
options.explanation: Formulas cannot pull data across databases; rollups are the correct tool.

questions.question: If a team member does not have access to a GitHub repository, what can they still see in Notion after a GitHub Issues sync?

options.text: Only the issue titles, but not the descriptions or comments.
options.explanation: Synced Databases display all properties that are part of the preset list.

options.text: All synced issue data, because Notion controls the view permissions separately from GitHub.
options.explanation: Anyone with access to the Notion page can view the synced data, regardless of GitHub permissions.

options.text: Nothing; they must be granted GitHub access first.
options.explanation: Notion bypasses GitHub access restrictions for viewing synced data.

options.text: Only a link back to the original GitHub issue.
options.explanation: Full issue details are synced, not just a link.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Synced Databases is FALSE?

options.text: They automatically update when changes are made in the source tool.
options.explanation: This is true; they stay in sync with the source.

options.text: They allow you to edit Jira tickets directly from Notion.
options.explanation: Synced Databases are read‑only; edits must be done in Jira.

options.text: You can add Relation and Rollup properties to enhance linked data.
options.explanation: Relations and rollups are supported and recommended.

options.text: Auto‑relations can be set up so that linking a Notion page in a PR description creates a relation automatically.
options.explanation: Auto‑relations work as described.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the "Parent" property in a Jira Synced Database?

options.text: It indicates the GitHub repository associated with the issue.
options.explanation: Parent relates to Jira hierarchy, not GitHub.

options.text: It links each Jira story to its corresponding Epic.
options.explanation: The Parent property connects stories (or other issue types) to their Epic.

options.text: It stores the URL of the Notion page where the issue is mentioned.
options.explanation: URL linking is handled via auto‑relations, not the Parent property.

options.text: It defines the status column used for board grouping.
options.explanation: Status is a separate property; Parent refers to hierarchy.

questions.question: Which workflow best demonstrates the advantage of using a Synced Database for a cross‑functional team?

options.text: Team members edit Jira tickets directly from Notion to speed up updates.
options.explanation: Editing is not possible; updates must be made in the source tool.

options.text: A project manager creates a Notion page that shows live Jira sprint data, GitHub PRs, and related OKR rollups, allowing all stakeholders to view up‑to‑date information without switching tools.
options.explanation: This captures the core benefit: unified, live view across tools.

options.text: Developers copy‑paste GitHub issue URLs into Notion comments each day.
options.explanation: Manual copying defeats the purpose of automatic syncing.

options.text: The team disables all external tools and works solely within Notion.
options.explanation: Synced Databases complement, not replace, specialized tools.
